text: The patient is positioned supine with a bump under the right abdomen, and the operating table is tilted to ten to fifteen degrees in reverse Trendelenburg position.
A diagram displays the port placement for the robotic hepatectomy, with black dots indicating the robotic ports and blue dots representing the assist ports.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic CUZA instrument through the assist port located at the right subcostal midclavicular line, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares for specimen extraction, which is typically performed through a fan and steel incision or by extending the umbilical port site.
The surgeon begins the hilar dissection by performing a cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder to expose the hilar structures for further dissection.
The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides the cystic duct and cystic artery, followed by lowering the hilar plate.

The surgeon encircles the cystic duct stump with an endo loop, retracting the bile duct medially to expose the posterior lateral liver hilum. The right hepatic artery is identified and circumferentially dissected.
The surgeon occludes the right hepatic artery and uses ultrasound with Doppler to confirm the patency of the left hepatic artery before proceeding with division.
The surgeon ties the right hepatic artery with a silk tie, clips it, and divides it, revealing the underlying right portal vein for further dissection.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the right portal vein, revealing its structure and surrounding tissue within the liver hilum.
The surgeon isolates and divides the branches leading to segment six and any caudate branches of the liver, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon ties the portal vein, ensuring that the portal vein bifurcation is not compromised during the procedure.
The surgeon clips the portal vein with endoclips and divides it, completing the hilar dissection and achieving extrahepatic inflow control.
The surgeon performs retrohepatic caval dissection, carefully separating the retrohepatic inferior vena cava from the liver tissue.
The surgeon divides multiple short hepatic veins, sequentially dissecting and controlling them with hemlock clips or dividing them using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon ties, clips, and divides a larger inferior right hepatic vein, ensuring proper vascular control during the hepatectomy.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the base of the right hepatic vein is visible, preparing for its division during the parenchymal division phase of the hepatectomy.
The surgeon marks the planned parenchymal division plane on the liver, indicating the area where the liver tissue will be divided during the hepatectomy.
The middle hepatic vein, previously identified and marked with ultrasound, is preserved during the parenchymal division, ensuring its integrity amidst the surrounding liver tissue.
Indocyanine green is administered after achieving inflow control, highlighting the demarcation line between the liver segments.
The left hepatic lobe appears bright due to the uptake of indocyanine green, while the right hepatic lobe remains dark, indicating the demarcation between the two lobes.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using a laparoscopic CUSA and a robotic vessel sealer, aiming to minimize blood loss during the division of liver tissue.
The surgeon uses the laparoscopic CUSA to dissect intrahepatic vascular and biliary structures, ensuring precise control of these elements during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the right hepatic duct intraparenchymally, with the surrounding liver tissue visible during the transection.
The surgeon carefully positions the instruments to avoid the hepatic duct bifurcation, ensuring there is no impingement on the left hepatic duct during the procedure.
The surgeon encircles and divides the right hilar plate containing the hepatic duct using a robotic stapler equipped with a vascular staple load, ensuring precise separation of these structures.
The surgeon dissects the right hepatic vein intraparenchymally and secures it using a robotic stapler.
The surgeon completes the parenchymal division of the liver, marking the end of this phase of the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgical specimen is placed into a large endobag and extracted through a fan and steel incision.
The surgeon inspects the liver remnant, which appears well vascularized, indicating healthy blood supply to the remaining tissue.
The surgeon uses Doppler ultrasound to confirm hemostasis and assess the inflow of the left hepatic artery and left portal vein, ensuring proper blood flow in the remaining liver tissue.
